items.q: Where does the verb go after 'weil' (because)?
items.correct: At the end of the clause.
items.misconception: Second position, as in main clauses.
items.why: Students apply V2 everywhere. Subordinating conjunctions (weil, dass, wenn, ob, etc.) send the verb to the END: 'Ich bleibe, weil ich müde BIN' (not 'weil ich bin müde').

items.q: How do you say 'I like school because the teachers are nice' in German?
items.correct: Ich mag die Schule, weil die Lehrer nett sind.
items.misconception: Ich mag die Schule, weil die Lehrer sind nett.
items.random_wrong: Ich mag die Schule, weil sind die Lehrer nett.
items.why: Classic subordinate clause error. After WEIL, DASS, WENN, OB, the verb goes to the END of the clause. 'Weil die Lehrer nett SIND' - sind moves to the end. This rule is extensively tested.

A famous faux ami. German 'Gift' = poison; English 'gift' = das Geschenk. The same Germanic root diverged - in English it stayed as 'something given', in German it became specifically a poisonous gift.

items.q: When was the last German nuclear reactor switched off?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: April 2023. “2011” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does EEG stand for in policy?
items.why: It's Renewable Energy Act. “European Energy Group” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What year is Germany's climate neutrality target?
items.why: The real figure is 2045. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
